author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
śrī⋅kṛṣṇa seviba bali’, gṛha chāḍi’ kuñje cali’,
jāite haya ‘abhisārī’ sakhi
kuñjasajjā kare jabe, ‘vāsakasajjā’ hana tabe,
‘utkaṇṭhitā’ kṛṣṇa-patha lakhi’

kāla ullaṅghiyā hari, bhoga-cihna dehe dhari’,
āile hana ‘khaṇḍitā’ takhana
saṅketa pāiyā baise, tabu kānta nā āise,
‘vipralabdhā’ nāyikā ta’ ha’na

manera kalahe hari, jāna cali’ duḥkha kari’,
‘kalahāntaritā’ santāpinī
mathurāte kānta gela, bahu-dina nā āila,
‘proṣita⋅bhartṛkā’ kāṅgālinī

nijāyatte kānte peye, krīḍā kare kānta laye,
‘svādhīna⋅bhartṛkā’ se ramaṇī
nāyikāmātrera haya, ei aṣṭadaśodaya,
vipralambha⋅sambhoga⋅bodhinī
en_translation:
Thinking, "I will serve Śrī Kṛṣṇa," leaving home and going to the grove, she becomes an 'abhisārī'. When she decorates the grove, she then becomes a 'vāsakasajjā', and when watching for Kṛṣṇa's path, she is an 'utkaṇṭhitā'.

When Hari arrives late, bearing the marks of enjoyment on His body, she then becomes a 'khaṇḍitā'. When she waits after receiving a signal but her beloved does not come, she becomes a 'vipralabdhā'.

In a quarrel of the mind, Hari goes away sorrowfully; she is a 'kalahāntaritā', full of distress. When her beloved goes to Mathurā and does not return for a long time, she becomes a 'proṣita-bhartṛkā', a pauper in love.

Having her beloved under her control, she plays with him; that woman is a 'svādhīna-bhartṛkā'. These eighteen states arise for every heroine, illuminating both separation and union.
